frameworks.name: Bounded Evidence Accumulation
frameworks.one_line: Noisy evidence samples accumulate to threshold; extended here to value-based choice.

frameworks.name: value-based decision making
frameworks.one_line: Preference choices treated as sequential sampling over fluctuating subjective values.

frameworks.name: perceptual decision making
frameworks.one_line: Contrasted as the case where temporally uncorrelated noise is reasonable.

core_question: Are subjective values stable over a sequence of value-based decisions, or do internal-state fluctuations leave detectable traces in choices?
key_assumptions: Subjective values depend on the decision maker's internal state
Internal states (and hence values) may change or undergo revaluation over time
Choice inconsistencies have been attributed to temporally uncorrelated noise on stable values
manipulations: Sequence of value-based decisions over familiar items
Comparison of value-based vs perceptual decision regimes
key_findings: Argues that temporally uncorrelated noise assumption is unreasonable for value-based decisions
Posits that hypothetical value changes should be detectable in sequential choice patterns
interpretations: Value construction via sequential sampling can account for serial dependencies in preference choices
Choice inconsistencies reflect drifting subjective values rather than only stable-value noise
